name: Solids and Nets — Boxes You Can Unfold
whatIsIt: A SOLID (3D shape) has length, breadth AND height — like a cube, cuboid, cone, cylinder, or sphere. Its flat surfaces are FACES, the lines where faces meet are EDGES, and the corners are VERTICES. A NET is a solid unfolded flat — the shape you could cut out and fold back into the box.
whyNeeded: The world is 3D: boxes, cans, balls, dice, ice-cream cones. Knowing faces, edges and vertices helps us describe and build them; nets are exactly how cartons and packets are designed and printed flat before folding.
realLifeUses: A cardboard carton is folded from a flat net
A dice (cube) has 6 faces, 12 edges, 8 corners
A tin can is a cylinder; an ice-cream cone is a cone
Wrapping a gift box is covering all its faces

vocabulary.meaning: A shape with length, breadth and height, like a cube or ball.
A flat surface of a solid (a cube has 6).
The line where two faces meet (a cube has 12).
A corner where edges meet (a cube has 8). Plural: vertices.
A solid unfolded into a flat shape that folds back into it.

story.title: Unfolding the Diwali Mithai Box
story.text: Meena's Diwali mithai came in a cube-shaped box. The halwai carefully unfolds it flat — six squares joined in a cross. 'That flat shape is the NET of a cube.' He counts on the folded box: 6 flat FACES, 12 EDGES where they meet, and 8 corner VERTICES. 'A flat net folds back into a solid box — that is how every carton is made!'
story.extractedProblem: A cube-shaped mithai box is unfolded flat. How many square faces make up its net?
story.answerInStory: 6 squares — a cube has 6 faces, so its net is 6 squares.

visual.caption: Spin the cube to count its 6 square FACES, 12 EDGES and 8 VERTICES. Unfold it and you get a net of 6 squares.

teachingGallery.title: Solids and their nets
teachingGallery.note: A net is a solid unfolded flat. Count the faces to know the shape.

examples.caption: 6 square faces fold up into a cube.
A square pyramid: 1 square base + 4 triangle faces.
A cylinder: 2 circle ends + 1 rolled rectangle.

standardMethod.summary: Describe a solid by counting its faces, edges and vertices; a net is the solid unfolded into flat faces.
steps: FACES: count the flat surfaces (a cube/cuboid has 6).
EDGES: count the lines where two faces meet (a cube has 12).
VERTICES: count the corners (a cube has 8).
NET: imagine unfolding the solid — every face lies flat, joined along edges.

alternateMethods.name: Handle-a-box
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To count faces, edges, vertices reliably.
steps: Hold a real box or dice.
Touch and tally each flat face, then each edge, then each corner.
Mark them so you don't count twice.

alternateMethods.name: Fold-test a net
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To check if a flat shape is a valid net.
steps: Check no two faces overlap and none is missing.
If it closes into the solid with no gaps, it's a valid net.
alternateMethods.example: Six squares in a cross fold into a cube.

workedExamples.problem: How many faces does a cube have?
steps: A cube is a box with square sides.
Count them: top, bottom, and four around.

workedExamples.problem: How many vertices (corners) does a cube have?
steps: Look at the corners of a box.
4 corners on the top, 4 on the bottom.

trickPractice.intro: Unfold the solid: a cube is 6 faces, 12 edges, 8 corners — count them on the net.

questions.q: How many faces does a CUBE have?
How many edges does a cube have?
hintLadder: Edges are where two faces meet.

questions.q: How many corners (vertices) does a cube have?
A cuboid (box) has how many faces?
hintLadder: Same as a cube, but rectangles.

questions.q: A square pyramid has how many faces?

commonMistakes.mistake: Mixing up faces, edges and vertices — saying a cube has 6 edges.
commonMistakes.fix: FACE = flat surface (6 on a cube), EDGE = line where faces meet (12), VERTEX = corner point (8). Touch a box: surfaces vs lines vs corners.

commonMistakes.mistake: Calling a cube a 'square' or a sphere a 'circle'.
commonMistakes.fix: A square is FLAT (2D); a cube is SOLID (3D). A circle is flat; a sphere is a ball. Solids have height you can hold.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking a shape with too few faces can fold into the solid.
commonMistakes.fix: A net must have EXACTLY as many faces as the solid — a cube net needs all 6 squares, no more, no fewer.

easy.q: How many faces does a cube have?

hintLadder: 3D means it has height you can hold.
A square is flat; a cube is a box.
easy.explain: A cube is a 3D solid; square and circle are flat.

medium.q: How many vertices (corners) does a cube have?
How many edges does a cube have?
hintLadder: 4 on top, 4 on bottom, 4 standing up.

challenge.q: A cube net is made of how many squares?
hintLadder: A net is the cube unfolded.

challenge.q: A tin can is shaped like a...
hintLadder: Round with flat top and bottom.
Not a cube, not a ball.
challenge.explain: A tin can is a cylinder.

hintLadder: It has height you can hold.
A ball, not a flat circle.

questions.q: A cube unfolded flat is called its...
questions.explain: The unfolded flat shape is a net.

teachBackPrompt: Find a real box (matchbox or dice) at home. Teach someone to count its FACES, EDGES and VERTICES by touching each, then carefully unfold a paper box to show its NET.

revisionCard.summary: Solids are 3D (length, breadth, height). Cube: 6 FACES, 12 EDGES, 8 VERTICES. A NET is the solid unfolded flat. Don't confuse flat (square) with solid (cube).

gameMission.title: Unfolding the Sweet Box
gameMission.brief: Count faces, edges and vertices, and match each solid to the net it folds from.

realLifeProject: Carefully open up an empty toothpaste or biscuit carton along its folds and lay it flat. That flat shape is its net — count how many faces it has.
